edges.description: Abbott secures re-election with polling advantage, Republican registration edge, and Trump endorsement. Would become second Texas governor to serve four terms (after Allan Shivers).

sources.title: Gubernatorial Trial Ballot (June 2026)

edges.description: Abbott has committed to expanding the $1 billion ESA voucher program signed in 2025, pursuing further property tax cuts, and maintaining Operation Lone Star border enforcement. He signed TRAIGA (comprehensive AI regulation) in 2025 and has taken hands-off stance on data center moratoriums.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ
'Mad mom' targets Greg Abbott

edges.description: Abbott has called SB 2 (2025) 'the largest day-one voucher launch in the country' and is expected to seek increased funding caps. Would accelerate private school enrollment growth and public school district fiscal stress, particularly in rural areas.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ

edges.description: Abbott will be term-limited in 2030. U.S. Senator John Cornyn faces re-election 2028; Abbott has not ruled out federal office. Lieutenant Governor Dan Patrick has also signaled gubernatorial interest, creating potential succession competition.

sources.title: Gubernatorial Trial Ballot (June 2026)

edges.description: Texas governors appoint judges to vacancies with Senate confirmation. Abbott has appointed 9 of 9 Texas Supreme Court justices and would continue shaping judiciary through 2030, including potential replacements for aging Republican-appointed justices.

sources.title: Texas Constitution Article 4, Section 11

edges.description: Abbott has refused Medicaid expansion throughout his governorship, calling it 'broken and bloated.' Texas maintains nation's highest uninsured rate. No change expected; rural hospital closures likely to continue.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ

edges.description: Without Medicaid expansion, rural hospitals face continued reimbursement shortfalls. Abbott has directed $239 million to rural psychiatric beds and cites $1.4 billion in federal rural health funding, but structural deficits persist.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ

edges.description: Abbott has spent $11+ billion on Operation Lone Star since 2021 and requested full federal reimbursement. Program includes National Guard deployment, border barrier construction, and state-level immigration enforcement.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ

edges.description: Texas SB 4 (2023) making illegal entry a state crime is under Fifth Circuit review. Abbott signed law; DOJ and ACLU sued. Final resolution affects state enforcement scope and potential copycat legislation in other states.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ

edges.description: Hinojosa would be first Democratic Texas governor since Ann Richards (1991-1995) and first Latina governor. Would require massive turnout surge, particularly among Latino and college-educated voters where she shows strength (49%-43% among Latinos, 50%-41% among degree-holders per June polling).

sources.title: Gubernatorial Trial Ballot (June 2026)
'Mad mom' targets Greg Abbott

edges.description: Hinojosa has pledged Medicaid expansion to insure 'about a million Texans,' repeal of SB 8 abortion restrictions, $15,000 teacher pay raises, and using $11 billion in unpaid Operation Lone Star funds for targeted property tax relief. Faces Republican legislative supermajorities in House and Senate.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ
'Mad mom' targets Greg Abbott

edges.description: Hinojosa's resignation from HD-49 (Austin) triggers special election per Texas Election Code Section 203.001. Governor calls election; winner serves remainder of term through 2028. Seat is safely Democratic (Austin/Travis County).

sources.title: Texas Election Code Section 203.001

edges.description: Potential candidates include Travis County Democrats with established records: Austin City Council members, former Hinojosa staff, or progressive activists. Special election timing affects 2027 legislative session participation.

Hinojosa has made Medicaid expansion central to campaign, framing it as 'reclaiming resources residents have already contributed.' Requires legislative appropriation and federal waiver; Republican legislature has blocked for 12+ years.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ

edges.description: Medicaid expansion requires 2-year budget appropriation. Republican legislative leaders have opposed; potential for special session, line-item veto battles, or compromise on limited expansion with work requirements.

Hinojosa was 'most visible voucher antagonist' in House, voting against SB 2 and authoring Fully Fund Our Future Act. Has pledged to oppose voucher expansion; full repeal unlikely without legislative majority.

sources.title: Issues — How They Differ

edges.description: First-term Democratic governor in Republican state faces structural headwinds. Success depends on Medicaid expansion delivery, economic conditions, and 2028 presidential coattails. Potential Republican challengers include Attorney General Ken Paxton, Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick, or U.S. Rep. Dan Crenshaw.

Dixon polls at 3% with no path to plurality in plurality-vote system. Victory would require both major candidates below 30% and unprecedented Libertarian surge, or successful legal challenge to Texas's sore loser law if Dixon previously filed as Republican/Democrat.

sources.title: Gubernatorial Trial Ballot (June 2026)

edges.description: Dixon platform includes universal school choice, broad deregulation, spending cuts, and civil liberties priorities. As governor with no legislative base, would rely on veto power and executive orders; appointments would emphasize libertarian credentials.

Libertarian governor with Republican legislature: potential cooperation on deregulation and tax cuts, but conflict on social issues, immigration enforcement, and abortion restrictions. Budget process would be highly contentious.

Paxton defeats Talarico, flipping a seat held by Cornyn since 2002. Victory would give Texas two Republican senators aligned with Trump, with Paxton replacing a traditional conservative with a populist nationalist.

sources.title: Poll: Paxton and Talarico are virtually tied in Senate race

edges.description: Texas Constitution Article IV, Section 12: When AG resigns to take another office, governor appoints successor to complete term. Gov. Greg Abbott would appoint Republican state Sen. Mayes Middleton, who leads Democrat Nathan Johnson by 5 points per June 2026 polling for the 2026 AG election.

sources.title: Poll: Paxton and Talarico are virtually tied in Senate race
Texas Constitution - Article IV

edges.description: If appointed, Middleton would seek election to a full four-year term in November 2028. Texas AG elections occur in midterm years; no Senate seat is on ballot in 2028.

Based on AG tenure and campaign positions: Paxton would support bills to expand domestic oil/gas production, oppose climate mandates, back abortion restrictions at federal level, and oppose LGBTQ civil rights expansions. Would likely join Senate Judiciary Committee given legal background.

sources.title: James Talarico, Ken Paxton launch Texas ads in U.S. Senate race

edges.description: Paxton's national profile and Trump alliance could position him for 2028 presidential run if Trump does not run. However, ongoing FBI investigation and impeachment history create significant electability concerns in general election.

Freshman senators typically receive lower-tier committee assignments, but Paxton's AG experience and relationships with Cruz/Cornyn could accelerate placement on Judiciary (immigration, judicial nominations) or Energy and Natural Resources. Would support Cruz's anti-immigration reform stance.

Senate Rules require ethics review of members under federal investigation. FBI investigation into Paxton's alleged misuse of office for Nate Paul could trigger Ethics Committee inquiry, potentially limiting committee assignments or requiring recusals on DOJ-related votes.

sources.title: James Talarico, Ken Paxton launch Texas ads in U.S. Senate race

edges.description: Talarico defeats Paxton, becoming first Democratic Texas senator since Bob Krueger (1993) and only the second since 1970. Would flip seat to Democrats, giving them potential 51-seat majority if other races break favorably. Would be youngest senator at 37.

sources.title: Poll: Paxton and Talarico are virtually tied in Senate race

edges.description: Texas Election Code Section 203.001: When state representative resigns to take another office, governor must call special election within 20 days to fill unexpired term. HD-50 (Round Rock/Pflugerville) is competitive: Biden+12 in 2020, but Talarico won by 14 points in 2022.

sources.title: Texas Election Code - Chapter 203

edges.description: Potential Democratic candidates: former HD-50 candidate Jennifer Skidonenko, Travis County Commissioner Ann Howard, or Round Rock ISD trustee. Republicans would target seat given 2024 rightward shift in suburban Texas districts.
Winner of special election would stand for full two-year term in November 2028. Redistricting after 2030 census could alter district boundaries.

Based on legislative record and campaign: Talarico would support Medicare for All who want it, Green New Deal-style climate investments, PRO Act labor protections, and immigration reform with pathway to citizenship. Would likely seek Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee.

sources.title: James Talarico, Ken Paxton launch Texas ads in U.S. Senate race

edges.description: Talarico would face reelection in November 2028, a presidential year with expected high Republican turnout in Texas. Would need to maintain suburban gains while defending against 'extremist' attacks on his progressive record. Potential GOP opponents: Rep. Dan Crenshaw, former Rep. Will Hurd, or AG Mayes Middleton.

At 37, Talarico would be youngest senator; could build national profile through youth/religious left coalition. However, 'Six-Gender Jimmy' attacks and progressive record create electability questions for national ticket. More likely VP prospect than presidential nominee.

Talarico victory would indicate Democratic surge in suburban Texas, potentially affecting downballot races: AG (Middleton vs. Johnson), Comptroller (Huffines vs. Eckhardt), and competitive state House districts. Could improve Democratic chances of breaking GOP supermajority in Texas House.

sources.title: Poll: Paxton and Talarico are virtually tied in Senate race

edges.description: Texas constitutional officers (Governor, Lt. Governor, AG, Comptroller, Land Commissioner, Agriculture Commissioner) serve four-year terms with 2026 elections. Next elections: 2030. However, special elections or resignations could create openings.

sources.title: Texas Constitution - Article IV

edges.description: Dan Patrick secures fourth term as Lieutenant Governor, continuing 11-year tenure. Holds over $30 million in campaign funds and won March 2026 primary by widest margin since taking office.

sources.title: State Rep. Vikki Goodwin to face GOP Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick
Texas Lt. Gov race: Goodwin, Vélez head to runoff

edges.description: Patrick presides over Senate with expanded Republican majority. Controls committee assignments, bill referrals, and floor agenda. Has pledged universal school choice/ESAs, property tax reduction through exemptions and rate caps, and expanded natural gas production with state intervention if needed.

sources.title: Dan Patrick policy positions
Texas Lt. Gov race: Goodwin, Vélez head to runoff

edges.description: Patrick, age 76 in 2028, could seek fifth term, run for governor if Greg Abbott does not seek re-election, or retire. Has not publicly announced post-2026 plans. Succession: If Patrick vacates office mid-term, Governor appoints replacement per Texas Constitution Article 4, Section 16.

sources.title: Texas Constitution Article 4

edges.description: If Patrick retires, 2028 Republican primary would likely feature state senators including Brandon Creighton (current Senate Education Committee chair), Charles Perry, or others from Patrick's allied stable. Democratic prospects would depend on 2026-2028 party rebuilding.

If Governor Abbott does not seek re-election, Patrick could run for governor. Would create open lieutenant governor seat. Attorney General Ken Paxton and others would likely compete. No public indication Patrick pursuing this path.

Patrick has made school choice/ESAs top priority. With Republican Senate majority and likely House support, universal ESA program probable. Would redirect public education funds to private/religious schools. Opposed by public school advocates.

sources.title: Texas Lt. Gov race: Goodwin, Vélez head to runoff

edges.description: If ESA program implemented, 2028 election debates would focus on program expansion or repeal. Public school funding shortfalls could become major issue. Teacher unions and parent groups would mobilize around 2028 legislative races.

Patrick campaigned on aggressive property tax reduction through exemptions and rate caps. Likely to advance further cuts, potentially constraining local government services. School districts particularly affected as property taxes fund education.

sources.title: Texas Lt. Gov race: Goodwin, Vélez head to runoff

edges.description: Patrick retains current office; no special elections triggered. Texas House District 47 (Goodwin's seat) remains held by whoever wins November 2026 election to replace her.

sources.title: Texas Election Code

edges.description: Vikki Goodwin elected Lieutenant Governor, becoming first Democrat in office since 1999. Faces Republican-controlled Senate likely to rewrite rules reducing her powers. House District 47 seat vacated.

edges.description: Reduced to largely ceremonial role or limited procedural functions. Would retain positions on Legislative Budget Board and Legislative Redistricting Board, providing some budget and redistricting influence. Could use platform for statewide advocacy on Medicaid expansion, public schools, and abortion access.

sources.title: Texas Lt. Gov race: Goodwin, Vélez head to runoff

edges.description: If Legislature fails to redistrict in 2031, Goodwin would serve on Legislative Redistricting Board with Governor, Attorney General, Comptroller, and House Speaker. Could influence congressional and legislative maps if deadlock occurs.

edges.description: Goodwin would co-chair Legislative Budget Board with House Speaker. Republican Speaker (likely Dade Phelan or successor) would control House delegation. Board develops state budget; Goodwin could advocate for Medicaid expansion and public school funding but would face Republican majority.

edges.description: Goodwin's election vacates Texas House District 47 (southwest Austin/Travis County). Governor must call special election within reasonable time; likely early 2027. District leans Democratic; 2018 Goodwin flipped seat from Republican Paul Workman.

edges.description: Given district demographics and Goodwin's progressive endorsements, likely replacement aligns with Texas Legislative Progressive Caucus. Would maintain or strengthen progressive bloc in House.

Goodwin campaigned on Medicaid expansion, repealing abortion ban, public school investment, Fair Rent Incentive Act, and water infrastructure. Would use lieutenant governor platform to advocate regardless of Senate constraints.

Texas allows legislators to hold another paid office; no vacancy created.
